content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

February 21, 1990

Michael J. Kersten
Deputy Legislative Counsel
Office of Legislative Counsel
3021 State Capitol
Sacramento, CA 95814

Re: Your Request For Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-90-054

Dear Mr. Kersten:

You have requested advice with respect to the mass mailing
provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Since you
are requesting advice in your capacity as legal advisor rather
than on behalf of a specific individual, we are treating your
request as one for informal assistance pursuant to
Regulation 18239(c).²

QUESTION

Are the three sample letters provided "substantially similar"
for purposes of the mass mailing restrictions of the Act?

CONCLUSION

The three letters are "substantially similar" for purposes of
the mass mailing restrictions. No more than a total of 200 copies
of the letters may be sent at public expense in a single calendar
month.

1 Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Government Code Section 83114; 2 Cal. Code of Regs.
Section 18329(c)(3).)

428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

Our File No. I-90-054
Page 2


FACTS

Your office is often requested to review proposed mailings on
behalf of members of the Legislature. You have enclosed three
sample letters and wish to know if these letters would be
considered "substantially similar" for purposes of the mass mail-
ing restrictions. (Section 89001; Regulation 18901, copy
enclosed.) Copies of the sample letters are attached. For
convenient reference, they are labeled #1, #2, and #3.

ANALYSIS

Section 89001 prohibits the sending of mass mailings at
public expense. The Commission has interpreted Section 89001 as
applying only to mailings containing the names or other references
to elected officers. The letters that you have provided reference
the elected officers who will be sending them by use of pronouns
such as "I" and "my" and would be signed by the elected officer.
If the letters will be sent at public expense to the recipient's
residence, place of employment or business, or post office box,
they fall within the prohibition on mass mailing unless one of the
available exceptions applies. (Regulation 18901(a).)

Section 82041.5 defines a mass mailing, in part, as "over two
hundred substantially similar pieces of mail...." (Emphasis
added.) Regulation 18901, adopted by the Commission on
December 13, 1989,³ defines "substantially similar" as follows:

(A) Two items are "substantially similar" if
any of the following applies:

(i) The items are identical, except for
changes necessary to identify the recipient
and his or her address.

(ii) The items are intended to honor,
commend, congratulate, or recognize an
individual or group, or individuals or groups,
for the same event or occasion; are intended
to celebrate or recognize the same holiday; or
are intended to congratulate an individual or
group, or individuals or groups, on the same
type of event, such as birthdays or an-
niversaries.


3 This regulation is in the rulemaking process. We advise that
the regulation be followed while it is undergoing review by the
Office of Administrative Law, and we anticipate that it will be in
effect in the near future.

Our File No. I-90-054
Page 3


(iii)  Both of the following apply to the
items mailed:

a. Most of the bills, legislation,
governmental action, activities, events,
or issues of public concern mentioned in
one item are mentioned in the other.

b. Most of the information
contained in one item is contained in the
other.

(B) Enclosure of the same informational
materials in two items mailed, such as copies of
the same bill, public document, or report, shall
not, by itself, mean that the two items are
"substantially similar." Such informational
materials may not include the elected officer's
name, photograph, signature, or any other reference
to the elected officer except as permitted
elsewhere in this regulation.

Regulation 18901(c)(3).

Since the letters provided are neither identical nor
congratulatory, they will only be considered substantially similar
if they meet the 2-part test set forth in subdivision
(c)(3)(A)(iii).

1. Do the letters mention most of the same bills, legisla-
tion, governmental action, activities, events, or issues?

All three letters mention the same governmental action to
equalize school funding in the state budget so as to give more
money to schools.

Letter #1: "During the recent budget hearings, my colleagues
and I passed a new funding system which will begin to equalize old
disparities. This new system will give [blank] schools an ad-
ditional [blank] million in funds next year."

Letter #2: "...I led an effort to equalize the funding for
categorical programs. This effort was successful, gaining ap-
proval by the Legislature as part of the budget hearings.
Students in [blank] can now expect an additional [blank] million
in funding for the 1989-90 school year."

Letter #3: "...I developed a proposal to begin to equalize
per pupil spending. My proposal will use some of the new money
approved by the voters with Proposition 98 to bring [blank]
schools up to the statewide average in categorical funding. My
proposal has been adopted....This will give [blank] about [blank]
more for our schools."

Our File No. I-90-054
Page 4


2.     Is most of the same information contained in both let-
ters?

     All three letters contain the following basic information:

     1.     Categorical programs/funding provide money for special
programs.

     2.     The money is not divided equally.

     3.     Los Angeles schools get about $1,000/student compared to
[blank] provided to [blank].

     4.     The new system will give [blank] money to [blank].

     The letters differ in some respects.

     1.     Letters #1 and #2 say that the categorical funding goes
to children with special needs such as the gifted or those with
English language problems. Letter #3 says the funds are used for
pet projects of special interests.

     2.     Letter #1 says the money goes mostly to large districts.
Letter #3 says the money goes to large, urban districts at the
expense of suburban districts. Letter #2 makes no similar direct
statement.

     3.     Letter #2 provides additional specific facts such as the
size of the education budget (over $24 billion), the number of
categorical programs in the state (75), and the state average
spending for categorical support ($464).

     Notwithstanding the above differences, we would
conservatively advise that most of the information in the three
letters is the same, and that the letters are therefore
substantially similar.

     Of course, this does not mean that the letters may not be
sent. It merely means that multiple copies of the letters may not
be sent so that the total sent in a calendar month exceeds 200.

     Alternatively, the information may be sent to unlimited
persons if all references to the elected officer are removed or if
no public funds are used to pay for the cost of distribution and
no more than $50 of public funds are used to pay for design,
production, or printing. (Regulation 19801(a)(3).)

Our File No. I-90-054
Page 5


I trust that this letter will be of assistance to you in
promulgating advice to Members of the Legislature. If you have
any additional questions in this regard, you may contact me at
(916) 322-5901.

Sincerely,

Kathryn E. Donovan
General Counsel


By: Margaret W. Ellison
Counsel, Legal Division

KED/MWE/aa

Enclosures
